vulnerability.title: Disable Kernel Parameter for Accepting ICMP Redirects by Default on IPv4 Interfaces
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects=0 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then # Comment out any occurrences of net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects from /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf files for f in /etc/sysctl.d/*.conf /run/sysctl.d/*.conf /usr/local/lib/sysctl.d/*.conf; do # skip systemd-sysctl symlink (/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf -> /etc/sysctl.conf) if [[ "$(readlink -f "$f")" == "/etc/sysctl.conf" ]]; then continue; fi matching_list=$(grep -P '^(?!#).*[\s]*net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects.*$' $f | uniq ) if ! test -z "$matching_list"; then while IFS= read -r entry; do escaped_entry=$(sed -e 's|/|\\/|g' <<< "$entry") # comment out "net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects" matches to preserve user data sed -i --follow-symlinks "s/^${escaped_entry}$/# &/g" $f done <<< "$matchin

triage_decision.reason: Disabling ICMP redirects is a standard network hardening practice that prevents certain types of man-in-the-middle attacks. Modifying sysctl parameters via /etc/sysctl.d/ is a non-disruptive way to apply these settings and does not impact system availability or authentication.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: networking affected; Potential disruption to network routing if the system relies on ICMP redirects for legitimate dynamic routing updates in a complex local network environment

execution_details.stdout:
# when using 1 interface for two networks when using NETKEY, the kernel
# thinks it can be clever by sending a redirect (cause it cannot tell
# an encrypted packet came in, but a decrypted packet came out),
# so it sends a bogus ICMP redirect
#
# We disable redirects for XFRM/IPsec
net.ipv6.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0
net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0
net.ipv4.conf.default.send_redirects = 0
net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0
net.ipv4.conf.all.send_redirects = 0
net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0

previous_attempts.scan_output: Single-rule scan failed for sysctl_net_ipv4_conf_default_accept_redirects (rule=xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_sysctl_net_ipv4_conf_default_accept_redirects). Could not verify.

llm_verdict.message:
The investigation reveals that the kernel parameter `net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects` is already set to `0` in the running configuration and is persistently configured in `/etc/sysctl.d/50-libreswan.conf`.

**Verification Steps Taken:**
1.  **Runtime Check:** Ran `sysctl net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects`, which returned `0`.
2.  **Persistence Check:** Searched configuration files using `grep -r "net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects" /etc/sysctl.conf /etc/sysctl.d/`, which identified the setting in `/etc/sysctl.d/50-libreswan.conf`.
3.  **Configuration Inspection:** Read `/etc/sysctl.d/50-libreswan.conf` to confirm the line `net.ipv4.conf.default.accept_redirects = 0` exists and is not commented out.

Since the requirement is already met, no changes were made to the system.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan is effective and follows best practices by using a dedicated file in /etc/sysctl.d/ rather than modifying the main sysctl.conf. This ensures clean management and persistence.
suggested_improvements: Consider also setting 'net.ipv4.conf.all.accept_redirects = 0' to ensure all interfaces (including currently active ones) are protected, as 'default' only applies to new interfaces created after the setting is applied.
